interventions.intervention_description: Participants were informed of a prior scientific study indicating that individuals who endorse social hierarchy and human dominance over nature consume higher quantities of red and white meat, whereas those who reject these structures consume more fruits and vegetables. Percentage of participants who reduced white meat consumption

paper_authors: Amiot, C. E.; El Hajj Boutros, G.; Sukhanova, K.; Karelis, A. D.
paper_title: Testing a novel multicomponent intervention to reduce meat consumption in young men

interventions.intervention_description: Participants received a presentation on declining meat consumption trends in Canada since 1980 and the negative health, animal welfare, and environmental impacts of meat consumption (including PETA videos). They completed a mind attribution task writing about a cow's thoughts and feelings, received practical tips for planning meat-free meals and making substitutions, and set a monthly goal to reduce, maintain, or increase their meat consumption.
interventions.intervention_mechanism: info: health, info: environment, info: animal welfare, norms: dynamic, perspective taking/individuation, goal pursuit: planning/pledge/reminder
interventions.control_description: Participants received information about how to complete dietary journals and were instructed to keep a record of their food intake, including condiments and beverages, over two weekdays and one weekend day; they were encouraged to write as much as possible. They were provided with food weight scales to estimate their portion sizes, and the first dietary journal was completed in the following week, representing a baseline measure of meat eating.

outcomes.outcome_description: Total meat consumption over two weekdays and one weekend day

interventions.intervention_description: Participants watched the "Lisa the vegetarian" Simpson's episode. paper_authors: Dakin, B. C.; Ching, A. E.; Teperman, E.; Klebl, C.; Moshel, M.; Bastian, B.
paper_title: Prescribing vegetarian or flexitarian diets leads to sustained reduction in meat intake

interventions.intervention_description: Participants were briefed on the rationale for and requirements of the diet for the adherence peroiod, they were also given a handout including the following information: "One type of diet that some people endorse is a Climatarian diet significantly reducing or eliminating red meat from one's diet. The reasoning is as follows: Livestock agriculture contributes up to a quarter of global greenhouse gas emissions, with almost all of this coming from cows and sheep. In Australia, producing 1 kg of beef requires an average of 15,182 L of water. Furthermore, livestock agriculture drives 80% of deforestation worldwide, using up a third of the world s ice-free land. Adopting a Climatarian diet is a powerful way to reduce your impact on the climate. Shifting to a Climatarian diet will reduce your carbon footprint by approximately 1000 kg of CO2 every year, or the equivalent of driving from Perth to Brisbane."

Participants were briefed on the rationale for and requirements of the diet for the adherence peroiod, they were also given a handout including the following information: "One type of diet that some people endorse is the ‘One step for the animals diet' eliminating chicken from our diets. The reasoning is as follows: Despite having the same capacity to suffer, farmed animals are denied the protection of animal cruelty laws that protect dogs and cats. This means that it is legal and widespread to cause immense suffering to animals raised for food.  If we can convince someone to stop eating birds, they would go from being responsible for the factory farming and slaughtering of more than two dozen land animals per year to fewer than one. Furthermore, while most cows and sheep raised in Australia spend most of their life grazing, the vast majority of chickens are raised in crowded factory farms."
interventions.intervention_mechanism: info: animal welfare, goal pursuit: efficacy/consequences/feedback

interventions.intervention_description: Participants were briefed on the rationale for and requirements of the diet for the adherence peroiod, they were also given a handout including the following information: "One type of diet that some people endorse is a Reducetarian diet reducing the amount of meat that we eat. The reasoning is as follows: Despite having the same capacity to suffer, farmed animals are denied the protection of animal cruelty laws that protect dogs and cats. This means that it is legal and widespread to cause immense suffering to animals raised for food. This includes painful surgical mutilations (without pain relief) and in most cases, intensive and unnatural overcrowding or confinement, and often a painful and traumatic slaughter. There are over 500 million animals confined like this in factory farms in Australia today. This includes cows, pigs, sheep and chickens. Adopting a reductionist diet is appealing because not everyone is willing to follow an all-or-nothing diet. It is also a powerful way to reduce animal cruelty. For example, halving your meat consumption will spare around 50 innocent lives every year. This includes about 13 land animals, and 37 fish and shellfish every year."
interventions.intervention_mechanism: info: animal welfare, goal pursuit: efficacy/consequences/feedback

paper_authors: Winkelmair, A.; Jansen, P.
paper_title: The positive impact of mindfulness interventions on the explicit and implicit affective attitudes toward vegetarian foods

interventions.intervention_description: Participants completed a 12-week compassion and caring-based training which consisted of weekly sessions á 75 min and weekly homework assignments. Participants were introduced to a way to offer love to themselves, other people, or animals. Affect dyad situations were applied where partners sit face to face, and one partner contemplates a situation to a specific theme of the main topics. The other partner listens attentively without giving any verbal or non-verbal feedback. Each session included a talk about a specific topic. The main subjects of this course were breath, handling difficult emotions, the four Buddhist Brahmaviharas, prosociality, compassion, appreciative joy, equanimity, forgiveness, gratitude, and self-compassion.

Participants completed a 12-week mindfulness-based stress reduction program that included various elements of meditation: body scan, mindful eating, emphasizing the breath, and sitting and walking meditation training. Every session contained a talk about specific topics such as different forms of meditation and problems that may arise with them. The focus of this mindfulness group was on the rather cognitive elements of mindfulness and its qualities of awareness. All sessions followed the same fixed sequence of quiet time, check-in, talk, meditation, exercise, homework, and check-out.

outcomes.outcome_description: Percentage of participants identifying as vegan or vegetarian

paper_authors: Pohlmann, A.
paper_title: The taste of compassion: Influencing meat attitudes with interhuman and interspecies moral appeals

interventions.intervention_description: Participants were asked to evaluate photographic art prints that a restaurant is considering exhibiting in its dining areas: participants viewed a slideshow consisting of 15 pretested stock photographs portraying scenes of vulnerability, human connection, care-giving, and displays of emotional distress. The photos pictured female and male adults, as well as children and the elderly, and a range of ethnicities. Prior to this, in both the control and treatment conditions, with a visible timer counting down from 3 min, participants were asked to recall 10 behaviors where they acted “particularly masculine/manly” or “particularly feminine/womanly” (depending on their gender). Participants read that men and women can easily recall 12 behaviors. The task is intentionally difficult with the purpose of leading participants to infer that their performance is below purportedly acceptable standards for their respective gender, thus threatening gender identity.
interventions.control_description: Participants were asked to evaluate photographic art prints that a restaurant is considering exhibiting in its dining areas: participants viewed a slideshow consisting of geometric shapes. Prior to this, in both the control and treatment conditions, with a visible timer counting down from 3 min, participants were asked to recall 10 behaviors where they acted “particularly masculine/manly” or “particularly feminine/womanly” (depending on their gender). Participants read that men and women can easily recall 12 behaviors. The task is intentionally difficult with the purpose of leading participants to infer that their performance is below purportedly acceptable standards for their respective gender, thus threatening gender identity.

interventions.intervention_description: Participants received text message twice a week, each falling into one or more of the following categories: (i) educational messages on the health (e.g., debunking nutritional myths), environmental (e.g., pointing out the need of reducing the consumption of animal products, food waste or unnecessary food packaging), and socio-economic (e.g., highlighting the necessity of supporting small stakeholders within the food system) consequences of eating behaviors; (ii) motivational messages to encourage sustainable diets, with tips on goal achievement; and (iii) recipes for simple yet tasty sustainable meals. In addition, participants received an online feedback session, in which a member of the research team with extensive knowledge of sustainable diets (UF) commented with the participants on the food consumption as registered during the last two evaluation weeks. The researcher provided tailored advice on improving the healthiness and sustainability of their diets and addressed any questions or concerns participants might have had regarding sustainable diets.

paper_title: Choice architecture promotes sustainable choices in online food-delivery apps

interventions.intervention_description: Participants were presented with a simulated delivery platform, with an additional tax imposed on items containing meat. The platform included nine restaurants that were based on real-world equivalents and offered a variety of popular cuisines. Each restaurant’s menu included a selection of starters, mains, desserts, and drinks (if applicable), to make the choice environment as realistic as possible. In total, participants could select from 164 unique food items for which we calculated the carbon footprint and calorie content. The menu page of a respective item displayed a red “T” icon and the text “Includes £X.XX of meat tax.” In the selection pop-up and in the checkout basket, an extra row identified the price added by the tax as “+£X.XX meat tax.” Additional rows displayed a small information icon, providing further details about the tax’s purpose and impact (“An additional meat tax has been added to this item to reflect its climate impact”) if hovered over with the mouse or touched on a mobile device.
interventions.control_description: Participants were presented with a simulated delivery platform in its regular “business as usual” design. They saw the same pages without carbon footprint or tax information, and both restaurant and menu pages were presented randomly.

interventions.intervention_description: Participants were presented with a simulated delivery platform, with additional information in the form of carbon-footprint labels included on the menu page of every food item and selection pop-up. The platform included nine restaurants that were based on real-world equivalents and offered a variety of popular cuisines. Each restaurant’s menu included a selection of starters, mains, desserts, and drinks (if applicable), to make the choice environment as realistic as possible. In total, participants could select from 164 unique food items for which we calculated the carbon footprint and calorie contentMeal items are rated based on their carbon footprint per kilogram using categories from A (very low) to E (very high), following the Global Carbon Budget for Food (2019 EAT-Lancet Commission).
interventions.control_description: Participants were presented with a simulated delivery platform in its regular “business as usual” design. They saw the same pages without carbon footprint or tax information, and both restaurant and menu pages were presented randomly.

interventions.intervention_description: The simulated delivery platform design that participants were presented with was altered to emphasize low-carbon alternatives. The platform included nine restaurants that were based on real-world equivalents and offered a variety of popular cuisines. Each restaurant’s menu included a selection of starters, mains, desserts, and drinks (if applicable), to make the choice environment as realistic as possible. In total, participants could select from 164 unique food items for which we calculated the carbon footprint and calorie content. Both the restaurant and menu pages were re-ordered, presenting options in decreasing order of sustainability, i.e., the lowest-carbon restaurants and meal items (within each menu category) were presented first.
interventions.control_description: Participants were presented with a simulated delivery platform in its regular “business as usual” design. They saw the same pages without carbon footprint or tax information, and both restaurant and menu pages were presented randomly.

outcomes.outcome_description: Proportion of meat or fish mains chosen

paper_authors: Jay, J. A.; D’Auria, R.; Nordby, J. C.; Rice, D. A.; Cleveland, D. A.; Friscia, A. R.; Kissinger, S.; Levis, M.; Malan, H.; Rajagopal, D.; Reynolds, J. R.; Slusser, W.; Wang, M.; Wesel, E.
paper_title: Reduction of the carbon footprint of college freshman diets after a food-based environmental science course

interventions.intervention_description: Students attended three 1-h lectures per week. The course covered general environmental science topics such as biodiversity, ecology, climate regulation, nutrient cycling, and the water cycle, carbon cycle, wind currents, atmospheric science, the mechanics of global warming, and histories of food production. Case studies were used to show the impacts of food systems on these processes and ecosystem services. As a writing assignment, students analyzed a peer-reviewed paper comparing the greenhouse gas emissions associated with various animal-based foods. Through classroom activities, students learned how to calculate the environmental footprint of meals using conversion factors from the Heller and Keoleian (2015) life cycle assessment (LCA) meta-analysis. Each student had to write a research paper on the carbon cost of producing a grocery store item of their choice, and then make a short film or performance to communicate something about their research findings for a general audience. Students also took midterm and final exams in both fall and winter quarters based largely on the Withgott and Laposata textbook Environment: The Science Behind the Stories (2014) and selected additional readings.

interventions.control_description: Participants attended two 75-min lectures per week (see SI for syllabus) covering a broad range of general science topics, including the Big Bang, stellar nucleosynthesis, planet formation, plate tectonics, biological evolution, genetics, and human evolution.

outcomes.outcome_description: Participants' reported how many servings of ruminant meat thye eat per week

interventions.intervention_description: A descriptive norm message was presented to guests by posters at the entrance of three dining halls (Mensa A, B, and C). The posters claimed that two out of three students in the respective dining hall ate a meat-free dish every day.

A stand was installed at the entrance of two dining halls (Mensa D and E) that exposed students to the injunctive norm message that eating meat was harmful to the environment and to animals, and that members of the ProVeg organization would approve of choosing a vegetarian over a meat dish. The stands were both set up in front of the dining hall entrances and were highly visible. Four members of the organization were present at any time and reached out to anyone interested in talking to them. In addition, bills and flags conveyed the norm message.

The sandwich department in the hypothetical retail store, established in a university lab, consisted of two refrigerators, placed back-to-back; only one was immediately visible to the participants as they entered the lab. paper_authors: Piernas, C.; Cook, B.; Stevens, R.; Stewart, C.; Hollowell, J.; Scarborough, P.; Jebb, S. A.
paper_title: Estimating the effect of moving meat-free products to the meat aisle on sales of meat and meat-free products: A non-randomised controlled intervention study in a large UK supermarket chain

interventions.intervention_description: The intervention was implemented in 20 stores on the week commencing January 27, 2019, for 12 weeks. The intervention consisted of moving 26 uniquely barcoded meat-free products within 4 meat-free categories (50% sausages, 30% burgers, 12% meatballs, and 8% mince) from their usual section into a newly created meat-free bay within the meat aisle. This new bay of meatfree products covered the whole bay, including top, middle, and bottom shelves. This meat free section replaced a bay where either meat was previously sold (16 stores) or where the bay previously contained chilled condiments (3 stores) or was unused (1 store)
interventions.control_description: In the control stores, meat-free products remained where originally located (usually in their own meat-free section outside the meat aisle) without changes to their position

outcomes.outcome_description: changes in (units/items sold) per store per week for intervention and control conditions

paper_title: Cooking up health: A novel culinary medicine and service learning elective for health professional students

interventions.intervention_description: Participants continued with the second component of "Cooking Up Health" intervention, intergrating service-learning experience in which participants applied their nutritional and socioeconomic knowledge by teaching at Chicago Public Schools, requiring students to co-lead at least four sessions of the "Small Bites" nutrition curriculum for elementary-age children. By synthesizing classroom learning with community service, students solidified their understanding of the complex relationships between food, health, and disease. In the first part of the program, participants had learned about six core pillars: applied nutrition science, hands-on plant-based cooking, disease management, patient coaching, cultural competency, and public health perspectives. Students had first completed the in-class learning component (six in-class didactic sessions) and, subsequently, completed the service component in the CPS classrooms after the last in-class learning component.

Participants were exposed to an updated version of the "Cooking Up Health" intervention, a six-session culinary medicine elective course with each session lasting approximately 2.5 hours. The curriculum is delivered to small groups of 10-12 students by a multidisciplinary team consisting of faculty facilitators and a chef instructor. It integrated six core pillars: applied nutrition science, hands-on plant-based cooking, disease management, patient coaching, cultural competency, and public health perspectives. For the updated version, the didactics were removed from the actual meeting time, and shifted to a series of 3–8 min videos students watched before class. In-person time was devoted to simulated patient coaching, student-led research discussions, and culinary sessions in the kitchen. Preparation for each class included videos, article review, and assignments totaled 60–90 min for cohort 2. In addition, the in-class learning component and the service component were completed concurrently in cohort two. For the service component, participants applied their nutritional and socioeconomic knowledge by teaching at Chicago Public Schools, requiring students to co-lead at least four sessions of the "Small Bites" nutrition curriculum for elementary-age children. By synthesizing classroom learning with community service, students solidified their understanding of the complex relationships between food, health, and disease.
interventions.intervention_mechanism: info: health, goal pursuit: cooking skills
